md: A **circle** is the set of all points in a plane that are the same distance from a central point. That distance is the **radius**, and the central point is the **center**. Circles are everywhere in geometry — and most of the vocabulary you'll need is just names for the parts.
**Center**: the point equidistant from every point on the circle.
**Radius** ($r$): the distance from the center to any point on the circle. Any line segment from the center to the circle is also called a radius.
**Diameter** ($d$): a line segment through the center connecting two opposite points of the circle. The diameter is twice the radius: $d = 2r$.
**Chord**: any line segment whose endpoints are both on the circle. The diameter is the longest possible chord.
**Arc**: a portion of the circle's circumference (the curve itself).
**Sector**: the pie-slice region bounded by two radii and an arc.
**Segment**: the region bounded by a chord and an arc.
Every circle obeys two beautiful formulas — both involving the constant $\pi \approx 3.14159$:
$C$ is the **circumference** — the distance around the circle. $A$ is the **area** — the region inside.
$\pi$ (pi) is the ratio of any circle's circumference to its diameter. It's the same number for every circle, regardless of size — that's the magic.
$\pi$ is irrational (its decimal goes on forever without repeating). Common approximations: $\pi \approx 3.14$ or $\pi \approx \frac{22}{7}$.

blocks.title: Try it
blocks.body: Adjust the radius slider in the widget. Watch how doubling $r$ doubles the circumference but **quadruples** the area. (Area scales by $r^2$ — a key fact.)

md: **Find area** of a circle with $d = 8$. First $r = d/2 = 4$. $A = \pi(4)^2 = 16\pi \approx 50.27$.
**Find radius** from circumference. If $C = 18\pi$, then $2\pi r = 18\pi \Rightarrow r = 9$.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Center, radius, diameter, chord, arc, sector, segment** — know the vocabulary. **$C = 2\pi r$** for circumference, **$A = \pi r^2$** for area. The diameter is twice the radius. Doubling the radius quadruples the area.
